title: Thierry Baudet: the Dutch nationalist out to take Europe by storm
text: As citizens across Europe head to the polls today, pundits are predicting a surge in support for the Continent's myriad populist parties as voters turn their backs on the Establishment. But few populist politicians have seen their support base swell so much as that of Thierry Baudet, the founder and leader of the Forum for Democracy (FvD), a controversial far-right party in the Netherlands that was formed just three years ago. Described by The Times as a populist upstart, the 36-year-old's outspoken views have seen the FvD poll neck-and-neck with Dutch President Mark Rutte's ruling liberal People's Party for Freedom and Democracy (VVD). The Guardian describes him as a philosopher-dandy with a penchant for playing piano in his office, posing nude on social media and quoting Latin in parliament. Left-leaning US news site Jacobin says the FvD is the most self-consciously elitist party in the Dutch political landscape. The FvD candidate list is made up of lawyers, surgeons, corporate managers, businessmen, retired military officers, and the party's programmatic statements promise class war from above on all fronts, the site adds. The New Statesman notes that the Dutch have experienced the same universal factors that tend to foster populism across the Western world, including deindustrialisation, a neoliberal consensus, social fragmentation and migration. Born in the Dutch town of Heemstede in 1983, Baudet studied law before working as a columnist for NRC Handelsblad, a liberal newspaper based in Amsterdam. His politics later veered to the extreme nationalist right, and he published books on anti-immigration and Eurosceptic themes. Europe was stunned when Baudet's FvD became the largest party in the Dutch senate at the provincial elections in March, ahead of the European elections in May. Latest polls suggested Baudet's party and Rutte's ruling VVD were level pegging, with both claiming around 15 percent of the vote share, with the Dutch Labour party (PvdA), GreenLeft and Christian Democratic Appeal following close behind at around 13 percent each. According to Politico, opponents of Baudet see him as a pseudo-intellectual whose sophisticated air masks extreme-right views. GreenLeft leader Jesse Klaver argued that Baudet fosters a sad old-fashioned view of men and women. University professor Amy Verdun believes that most voters will ultimately back centrist parties, due to the repeated failure of squabbling populist and far-right parties to unite within the European Parliament.

title: Macron's reckoning: The European Parliament elections in France
text: The European Parliament elections will be the first major test at the polls for Emmanuel Macron since being elected president in 2017. These elections come after six months of street protests that have derailed a reform programme the president is now anxious to get back on track. The elections are set to be a tightly fought battle for supremacy between Macron's centrist, liberal, pro-EU governing party LREM and Marine Le Pen's nationalist, populist, anti-EU party RN. Currently, the LREM and RN lists were neck-and-neck at around 22 percent of vote projections, way out in front of all others, with the RN nudging ahead in some recent polls. Since November 2018, Macron found his reform agenda derailed by a popular insurgency, the gilets jaunes protests, accompanied by the worst civil disorder seen in France for decades. The protests began against a planned fuel tax rise and grew into a wider movement against the political class. Macron conceded around 10 billion euros in pay rises and tax exemptions in December, followed by close to 10 billion euros more in tax cuts and pension rises in April, at the end of a great national debate that saw over 10,000 town hall meetings between January and March. Macron's approval ratings sank to the mid-20s during this period. Macron must ensure his Renaissance list finishes ahead of Le Pen's list to signal his recovery from the protests and avoid what is seen by many as a referendum on his presidency. Le Pen, for her part, needed her party's list to finish ahead of Macron's to match its 2014 European election performance of 24.9 percent and 24 seats. The centre-right Republicans occupied third place in projections at around 14 percent, with Jean-Luc Melenchon's far-left France Unbowed on 8 to 9 percent and the Greens on 7 to 8 percent. France's Socialists, in power under Francois Hollande from 2012 to 2017, were polling at barely 5 percent. Projected turnout was around 40 percent, which would break the record for these traditionally low-turnout elections in France. In recent polling, only 29 percent of French respondents saw the European project as a source of hope, down from 61 percent in 2003.
